prose: The seasoned mahogany beneath your palms feels less like a desk and more like the salt-crusted gunwale of an emigrant hull running before an Atlantic gale. Under the pads of your fingers, the grain is cool and hard, sealed with beeswax and the grease of anxious hands, yet the stacks of foreign reports thrum with a dry, static friction. Each letter from the Midwestern outposts is a sheet of pulped rag-paper that slides against the next with the dry, papery rasp of autumn leaves in the old country, a thin barrier holding back the weight of a people who have learned to live beneath the surface of the new republic.

A persistent, damp draft crawls along the floorboards, bypassing the heat of the stove to settle directly into the stiff joints of your fingers. It is the raw, biting frost of the uplands, a physical ache that recalls the meager Bavarian soil from which the {family_name} name first drew its strength, now grown so vast that its shadow covers millions who look to you for bread and silence. That stewardship is an ache that lodges between your shoulder blades—the dull, throbbing weight of a congregation that has buried its old tongue in the loam, their lives pressing against your skin through the ink-smeared fiber of these unread ledgers.

Your thumb traces the hard, jagged rim of the secular seal, the crimson wax dried to the brittle consistency of bone. The sharp imprint of the state’s signet bites into your calloused flesh, demanding that the old parish pieties bend to the cold, wet-iron logic of ledger-books and customs-tolls. The simple, raw devotion that once clothed your ancestors in rough-spun wool now feels like coarse sacking when the world demands silk; you must master the slick, greasy feel of minted coin and the dry slide of treaties if you are to keep this quiet flock from being ground to dust beneath the heels of progress.

Between your forefinger and thumb, the experimental synthetic weave feels unnervingly slick, like river silt or the skin of an eel, yet its weightless, rot-resistant thread promises a dry, clean shelter for those dying in the muddy swamp-havens of the new territories.

choice.text: Approve the new uniform material, prioritizing comfort and practical application in aid efforts.

prose: The heavy, grease-thick wool of the old-world looms rasps against your raw knuckles, reeking of mutton-tallow and peat-smoke—a coarse, heavy bite that has shielded the parish poor from the winter gales for two centuries without tearing.

choice.text: Insist on traditional materials, valuing continuity and symbolic strength over modern efficiency.

decision.prompt: A burgeoning humanitarian crisis in a distant land demands significant resources. How do you respond to the call for aid?

options.text: Mobilize all available resources, even if it strains existing projects, upholding the moral imperative.
Form an alliance with a secular international body, pooling resources but risking doctrinal compromise.

prose: The copper-heavy tolling of the gallery clock did not merely count the hours; it shuddered through the dry pine floorboards, a thick, percussive heartbeat that measured the slow expiration of the old electorate. Beneath that rhythmic thud vibrated the imagined roar of high-pressure cylinders three hundred leagues to the west, where your tracts on the restoration of the soul were flung wet from the iron cylinders into the soot-heavy air of industrial slums. This was an empire governed by a low, scraping acoustic—the dry slide of brass keys across desks slick with tallow, and the gravelly, dry-mouthed negotiations of attachés who spoke in the breathy, sibilant German of the court, knowing a whisper could devalue a state loan before the ink was even dry on the dispatch.

But against this low, domestic murmur rose a sharper, insectoid rattle from the courtyard wing—the relentless, iron-on-iron clatter of Remington typewriters from the new foreign trade commission across the cobblestones. It was a cold, staccato chatter, devoid of the scratch of goose-quill or the deep, chest-vibrating cadence of the Latin liturgy, yet it possessed the terrible velocity of a ledger-book state writing its modern scriptures in cold, carboned strike-notes. This was the voice of a rival house that did not pray to the old martyrs, a clack-clack-clattering engine hammering out concessions of coal-fields and steel-gauges designed to bury the quiet, centuries-deep authority the {family_name} name had carved into the dark Danubian clay.

In the valley below, past the convent walls and the ancient, lead-weighted spires, the heavy, rhythmic pant of a freight locomotive shook the leaded glass of the conservatory—a wet, roaring hiss of steam that grew more predatory with every turn of the season. It was the century’s real anthem, an iron-shod thunder that promised to crush any house that sat too long in the quietude of its own chapel. If your line was to survive this loud, secular leveling, your voice could not remain a chapel whisper; it had to borrow the iron throat of the press, shouting the old truths through the very smoke-stacks of the age before the silence of obsolescence settled over the valley.

Through the thick, dust-heavy velvet of the library drapes, the dry, clipped consonants of a Baltic maritime envoy grated from the map-room, his voice rising to an arrogant pitch as he tapped his brass pointer against the varnished charts of the new global trade-ways.

choice.text: Advocate for inclusion of ethical labor clauses in the maritime pact, leveraging your influence for broader social good.

prose: In the vaulted corridor, the rapid, boot-scuffed pacing of your land-agent reached you, accompanied by the dry, papery rustle of deeds as he muttered in the thick, throat-clearing dialect of the Alpine foothills over the tracts of the far eastern marshes.

choice.text: Prioritize direct land acquisition for community expansion, ensuring the physical foundation of your global presence.

thread.crossing: The sharp, metallic scratching of steel nibs against cold Bavarian vellum is answered by the dry, rhythmic whisper of a hair brush wielded by the agent of the {family_name}. They have come to swap access to the treaty-port presses of Canton for the rights to translate your family’s tracts, binding your house in a quiet, lucrative alliance that hums from the Danube to the Yangtze.

prose: The leaden dusk of the lakeshore city settles like soot over the slate roofs, viewed through the leaded panes of the high study. Below, the sprawling municipal grid ignites in a cold, blue-gold lattice of gas-lamps and early electric carbon filaments, tracing the quiet, massive drift of the {family_name} freight lines and steamships. It is a vast cartography of light, yet it holds the heavy, industrial chill of a territory that has forgotten the forest-quiet of the old country, traded for the unceasing hum of American steam and coal.

A towering, iron-girded elevator of glass and raw timber—the new grain exchange—glares back against the bloody smudge of the western horizon, its sheer verticality slicing through the modest brick gables of the immigrant parishes like a butcher’s cleaver. It boasts no saint’s name and carries no bells, yet its massive shadow stretches across the paved lanes, a modern temple built on Great Lakes grain and iron-ore credit. In this dimming, yellow glare, the parish boundary dissolves into the cold ledger of the yard, leaving your people to either master the machine or be crushed beneath its iron treads.

Beside that colossal engine of wealth, the dark needle of the cathedral spire rises, a sharp cross of copper-green tarnishing in the damp lake air, holding its silent vigil against the night. The stained glass of the high transept glows with a faint, dying ruby fire, reflecting the ancient, blood-bought liturgies that sustained your grandfather through the wilderness. To look upon these two titans—the soot-stained stack of the exchange and the silent, stone finger of the Church—is to see the twin horizons of your bloodline, a knot of iron and spirit that only your signature can sever or bind.
The blueprint for the transatlantic telegraph cable lies unfurled across the oak table, its white cyanotype lines cold and skeletal, mapping a new empire of wire beneath the ocean.

choice.text: Invest in a global, faith-bound communications network, stringing copper through the oceans to carry the old tongue and the holy word.

prose: Beneath the pale circle of the oil lamp, the hand-colored vellum of the old Bavarian parish atlas lies open, its frayed margins tracing the ancient, quiet paths of the forest missions.

choice.text: Consolidate your strength within the traditional parishes and wilderness missions, anchoring the old ways deep into the soil.

decision.prompt: The world grows smaller and louder, threatening to submerge the old identity in a sea of modern commerce. How will you steer the legacy through this great shifting of the earth?

options.text: Align with the rising industrial states, embedding your agents in their halls of power to guide their policies through moral leverage.
Retreat into the strength of your own cooperatives, proving the purity of your trade and the self-sufficiency of your parishes.
Launch a fierce spiritual crusade across the sea, sending out mission-ships to plant the cross in every harbor that buys your grain.

prose: The chicory-charred sludge on the back of your tongue is the same bitter brew that got your grandfathers through the dry years in the Palatinate—a peasant's draft, cut with roasted dandelion root to stretch the bean. It tastes of the sour, damp limestone of Bavarian cellars and the brine-cured pork of the emigration hulls. Every sip carries the dust of the old parish ledgers, where the {family_name} name was first scratched in iron-gall ink by parish priests who knew how to trade a bushel of oats for a soul's quiet compliance. This is the dark vinegar of survival, swallowed down in silence so that those who came after might finally sit at the tables of the great.

Yet underneath that harsh, scorched dreg rises the heavy fat of real Dutch cocoa, a velvety grease that coats the throat like the butter-soft crusts of a festival kugel. It is the taste of the cloister's wheat-loaf, sweet with yeast and the heavy, black-bread malt of monastery dornfelder that has nourished the destitute from the Munich suburbs to the crowded tenements of the Ohio Valley. This is the milk of the church's triumphs, bought not with armies but with the copper groschen of maids and weavers who tucked their small coins into the parish box, trusting that the great, silent oak of {family_name} stewardship would shade their graves in the dirt of a new hemisphere.

And still, the well-water from the parish cistern leaves a cold, rusty tooth-tap of iron on the palate, a gray soot-smudge of Silesian coal that clings to the roof of your mouth. It tastes of the copper-plate rollers that churn out the German-language hymnals for the diaspora, the wet lead of the print-shops, and the steam-engines hauling immigrants deep into the prairie's silence. This is the modern Host, dry and hard-bitten, a communion of gears and ledger-ink that demands a spine of cold steel under the heavy wool of the cassock. You drink it down to the cold sediment, knowing the world no longer yields to prayers alone, but to the iron-bound weight of the book.

The sudden, burning prickle of clove and dried orange peel on your gums—the hot, salt-rimmed grease of the Dutch cargo lines—brings the clean, metallic taste of Hamburg harbor to your mouth, reminding you of the thousands of Atlantic crossings your ledgers now command.

choice.text: Reflect on the economic engine driving the global mission, savoring the strategic power of wealth.

prose: A floury grain of coarse rye, sour and gray as the winter clay of the old country, lodges between your teeth, pulling your senses back to the drafty schoolrooms where the children of the poor still recite their catechisms in the mother tongue.

choice.text: Contemplate the spiritual sustenance provided by your institutions, focusing on the core mission of faith.

decision.prompt: The bitter chicory dregs cling to the roof of your mouth, a reminder of the grim compromises that bought this peace. As the shadow of the parish tower falls across the cold parlor, how will you resolve the future of the {family_name} name?

options.text: Accept the cold hand of the provincial tax-collector's daughter, as the diocese counsels, to fill the quiet halls of {surname} with legitimate heirs born to endure.
Keep your vow of solitary devotion, but take your late cousin's orphaned boy from the convent school to raise him as the sole, chosen heir to our spiritual estate.
Let the cup pass. Sign the entirety of the {surname} holdings over to the monastic order to build a lasting sanctuary, letting our name dissolve into the stone and incense.

prose: The subterranean habitat module smelled of dry lard, scorched copper, and the close, greasy heat of heavy-duty hydraulic presses—a scent that clung to {full_name}'s skin like the grit of an Ohio foundry. This was the engineered atmosphere of a pressurized deep-shaft testing facility, where the damp, river-bottom breath of the old family farmsteads had long since been traded for the dry, ozone-sharp draft of high-voltage transformers and zinc-plated conduit. Yet, tucked into the weld-joints of the steel framing, there lingered the ghost of something older: a faint, dry dust of wild marjoram and mountain sage, desiccating in a small wire mesh basket beside a rusted, unbranded iron crucifix. It was the scent of a lineage that had packed its faith into heavy tool chests, its devotion parching now in the cold, recirculated air of the facility.

Generations had dissolved since the first {family_name} mechanics had laid their hands upon the early American steam dynamos, their names quieted and scrubbed of their vowels during the great mid-century assimilation to escape the sudden, sharp suspicions of the war years. To {given_name}, this closed-loop atmosphere—inhaled, exhaled, and scrubbed a thousand times over through massive charcoal beds beneath the bedrock—was not a deprivation but a quiet, institutional testament. It was the breath of a collective legacy, measured out in millibars through heavy iron regulators, keeping the shifts alive in an underground cathedral of concrete and vacuum testing vaults.

Even the mid-shift meal, a pale yeast gruel and synthetic fats heated on a shared hotplate, gave off the heavy, scorched-grain steam of old griddle-bread, though spiked with the sharp, chemical preservatives of modern industrial rations. It was the smell of working-class survival made monumental—a machinist’s lunch boiled in a pressurized bunker, thick with the iron tang of the steam lines and the bitter oil of the exhaust pumps. {given_name} breathed the steam in deeply, letting the dry, hot vapor coat the back of the throat, finding in its sterile heat a familiar, stubborn dignity that refused to be scrubbed from the ledger of the living.

The air scrubbers groan in the overhead joists, heavy with the flat, damp moisture of six hundred technicians breathing the same recirculated air. You turn your fingers to the cold copper dials of the environmental manifold, seeking a scent-mix that mimics the soft, mist-laden mornings of the old northern forests, where the smell of wet pine and damp loam made the morning prayer rise as naturally as the fog.

choice.text: Calibrate the facility's environmental controls to prioritize spiritual solace.

prose: But beneath the familiar, greasy smell of the kitchen vents comes a sudden, ice-cold draft from the high-pressure seals—an odor of parched, iron-rich dust, dry as a limestone quarry and entirely unbaptized, whispering from the deep, dark vacuum chambers that wait just beyond the heavy bulkheads.

choice.text: Investigate the foreign drafts for scent-traces of the raw, unbaptized world outside.

prose: The low, rhythmic shudder of the station's iron boiler hummed directly through {given_name}'s boots, a persistent, bone-deep vibration that rattled the ankles and settled as a dull ache in the pelvis. This orbital cell was no silent sanctuary, but a trembling furnace of copper and steel, throbbing with the collective heat of families huddled beneath corrugated bulkheads. Every surface offered its own cold, greasy testimony: the slick face of the dataslate, the gritty film of machine oil on the brass railings, and the coarse, lanolin-heavy bite of the communal wool blankets that smelled of lye, vinegar, and old sweat. Up here, in the high-altitude dark, life was measured by the friction of raw skin against cold metal—a constant, tactile struggle to hold onto the warmth of the living.

A sudden, violent lurch—the heavy, metallic clonk of a coolant valve cycling in the bulkheads—sent a sharp tremor through the floorboards. {given_name} reached out instinctively, palms catching the icy, unyielding steel of the structural frame before tracing the familiar, oil-softened grain of the boxwood crucifix hanging in the corner. The wood felt remarkably warm, its edges worn smooth by generations of thumbs that had rubbed the feet of the tiny carved Savior into featureless nubs since the days of the old kingdom. That small, organic density was an anchor of gravity, a sharp contrast to the biting, synthetic freeze of the outer hull plates just inches from their neck, where the vast, unpeopled silence waited to drink their breath.

Every chore on the station was a lesson in the limits of flesh and steel. Calibrating the nutrient valves required a precise, clicking pressure of the thumb; sealing the rubber gaskets of the hydroponic tubs demanded a heavy, bruising lean of the shoulder; and navigating the unheated maintenance shafts meant dragging one's weight along frozen guide-wires that bit through the leather of their work gloves. The skin of their hands grew thick with calluses, yellowed by grease and scrubbed raw by chemical soap until their fingers resembled the roots of the pines they had left behind. It was a harsh, physical liturgy of survival, where faith was not merely whispered, but hammered and pressed into the very joints of their new world.

Your fingertips trace the icy, glass-smooth face of the primary navigation deck, feeling the faint, electric prickle of the long-range gyros humming beneath the brass casing as they hunt for the distant, cold peaks of their new horizon.

choice.text: Focus on the navigation and strategic positioning of the orbital station.

prose: You press your hand to the rough, grease-stained pigskin of the communal liturgy book, its spine split and soft from the damp, tallow-scented breath of a hundred kneeling pioneers who brought their God up into the high silence.

choice.text: Attend to the spiritual well-being and communal practices of the colonists.

decision.prompt: The dry, recycled air and the relentless rattle of the thrusters wear down the spirits of the faithful. A faction of young mechanics, their hands calloused by steel rather than soil, begins to murmur against the long hours of kneeling and traditional devotions, demanding the liturgy be adapted to fit the harsh shifts of the turbines. How do you answer their restless hands?

options.text: Reaffirm the absolute weight of the old liturgy, demanding they bend their knees to the traditional hours to keep their souls anchored in this weightless dark.
Offer a compromise that bends with the shifts, letting them shorten the devotions to match their maintenance rotations while preserving the sacred core.

prose: The station spoke in a wet, heavy rattle—the rhythmic, industrial sigh of the CO2 scrubbers shaking the thin tin bulkheads of the commons like drafty cabin walls in a winter storm. Beneath that mechanical heartbeat ran the real, quiet liturgy of the {family_name} line: not the loud, brass-rimmed hymns of the old world’s stone cathedrals, but a low, collective murmur of prayers rising from the damp hydroponic troughs where the children gathered pale, turnip-leaf greens from the volcanic slag. Every blow of an iron wrench in the grease-wells three decks down shivered up through the floorboards as a hollow, leaden *clonk*, sounding for all the world like a church bell rung underwater, keeping time for the soft, felt-shoed shuffle of pilgrims pacing the cold outer ring.

Then the high-gain comms horn hissed, spitting the dry, silver-plated syllables of a director from the consortium's regional office down into our silence. That voice had no lard in it, no salt of the soil, only the flat, clean-scraped efficiency of Earth-bound boardrooms and actuarial ledgers, slicing through the warm, grease-heavy air of our kitchen-decks to bark coordinates for the Hera-4 claim. It was an offense to the ear—a thin, paper-dry click of numbers that treated the immense, star-strewn wilderness of God as nothing more than a yard of unmeasured timber. Against the slow, round, pious vowels of our flock's whispered devotion, this sterile clatter was a warning that the market had hunted us even into the deep desert of heaven, hungry and unblessed.

A shudder ran through the iron plates, preceded by a low, sub-audible thud that set the pewter cups in the cupboards dancing—the muffled, thunderous bite of a thermal lance eating into a carbon-rock three leagues out. It was the sound of bread being torn from the stone, a violent, necessary roar that left a long, singing ring in the ears of every soul aboard. In the long hush that followed, the receivers on the bridge kept up their endless, dry crackle, capturing the hiss of cosmic dust against the dish like dry corn thrown against a frozen windowpane. Here, between the blast of our labor and the absolute silence of the void, we hung by a thread of copper and prayer, listening to the dark for any voice that might answer our own.

Through the rusted speaker horn, the gravelly, soot-choked voice of an elder begins the morning devotion, the ancient, half-forbidden words of the old country rising steady and heavy as an iron anchor through the cold air of the berths.

choice.text: Join the communal prayers, reinforcing the spiritual foundation of the colony.

prose: The shortwave receiver whistles with a high, icy whistle, spilling the dry, unblessed ledger-talk of the consortium's clerk three sectors over like dry grain falling onto a tin plate.

choice.text: Monitor the rival corporation's communications for strategic insights.

thread.crossing: Every strike of our brass wrenches in the grease-wells down below is answered by the high, rhythmic clink of the {surname} family's automated scrapers harvesting the ice-shelves just beyond our lower bulkheads. Their silent, silk-clad proxy from the shipping syndicate offers no greeting, only a cold ledger demanding a steeper tribute of yeast-distillate to keep the main air-loop running. Our hushed prayers for bread and survival are now swallowed by the grinding noise of their superior machinery looming directly above our heads.

prose: The double-paned quartz of the observation deck held back a silence so thick it seemed to grease the glass, broken only by the cold, violet wash of the gas giant rolling below like a drowned world. Out in that quiet deep, the stars did not shimmer; they were hard, salt-white chips of bone frozen against the black, casting long, clean-edged shadows across the hull's iron ribs. Farther still, the tiny, amber winks of the pioneer outposts looked no larger than the sparks from a blacksmith's hearth in the old country, small and frail enough to be snuffed by a breath, yet each one marking where a family under {full_name}’s care kept their silent, shivering watch.

Before {given_name}, the tactical terminal glowed with a shivering cobalt grid that threw up the outlines of their planned parish in the stars. The blue light caught the drift of mountain sage burning in the brass censer, turning the grey smoke into a mock-nebula that hung over the holographic chapel-spires and the low, heavy domestic blocks designed to mimic the stone barns of the Isar valley. Every line of the draft, weighed and approved by the {family_name} elders, was a defensive wall of straight lines and sharp corners—a desperate, pious geometry meant to lock out the formless void and build a village where the tongue and the old ways could survive without being swallowed by the vast, silent basin of this new home.

Then, an unexpected green stain bled across the cobalt map, pulsing like a wet leaf caught in a mill-race. A dark mass, heavy with the dull grey signature of cold iron and rich silicates, had drifted into the sweep; yet, tangled around its jagged flanks like threads of raw lamb’s wool, the pale, breathing silver of a stellar-drift migration glowed in slow, biological pulses. It was a sight that halted the breath—the raw, hard iron they needed to forge their plows, wrapped in the ghost-light of an ancient, unbaptized life that seemed to watch them from the dark, demanding a choice that would either crown their long pilgrimage or turn their new home into a common quarry.

The old priest’s face is a map of dry gullies in the blue glare of the terminal, his eyelids heavy as he traces the sign of the cross over the glass, his mouth moving in the silent, Bavarian Latin of his youth.

choice.text: Reflect on the spiritual significance of the stars and the divine purpose of their journey.

prose: Beneath the primary map, the secondary monitor displays the blocky, red-iron silhouettes of the heavy-bore drills, their mechanical teeth rendered in flat, unblinking lines of cold weight and hunger.

choice.text: Focus on the practicalities of resource acquisition and economic expansion.

decision.prompt: The newly discovered asteroid holds immense mineral wealth, crucial for the colony's growth. However, its exploitation would disrupt a unique cosmic ecosystem and a potential sacred site for a nascent alien intelligence, whose presence has been subtly detected. How will the {family_name} line approach this celestial dilemma?

options.text: Declare the asteroid a protected zone, prioritizing the sanctity of the potential alien site and cosmic ecosystem over immediate material gain.
Begin limited, sustainable extraction efforts, carefully balancing resource needs with environmental and potential diplomatic concerns.
Commence full-scale mining operations, asserting humanity's right to utilize resources for survival and expansion, despite external factors.

prose: The synthetic starch paste clinging to the roof of the mouth had always been a flat, gray penance, tasting of recycled sulfur and the dry nitrogen that had sustained {full_name} through the long, quiet generations of the crossing. Yet tonight, beneath the chalky residue of the daily ration, there came a sudden, sharp needle of green—a tiny sprig of real mountain dill, coaxed from the moist dark of a hidden tray, tasting of cold loam, manure, and stubborn Bavarian earth. It was a minuscule, salt-bitten liturgy on the tongue, a wild burst of alpine forest that cut through the decades of chemical grease to promise that the cold iron of the ship might yet be broken by the root.

The draft of reclaimed water that followed tasted thin and dead, carrying the copper chill of the scrubber plates and the stale breath of three hundred families who had learned to keep their native tongue behind closed teeth. With every swallow, {given_name} tasted the pale ghosts of the Isar and the Inn—not the rushing, silt-heavy rivers of the old songs, but a hard, limestone runoff scraped from the sweat of the bulkheads and the bottom of the iron cisterns. It was the salt of their long, invisible labor, a dry water that did not comfort so much as it preserved the bloodline for a soil they had never seen.

This was the bitter vintage of their quietest era, a communion of grease, starch, and the iron-shavings of tireless devotion. What they passed down to the heir of the {family_name} name was not a fat land of milk and hops, but this salt-bitten capacity to endure—to find the taste of the Creator in the dust of the turbines and the mold of the wet iron. It was the palate of a people who had survived by making themselves small in the dark, waiting for the day they could spit out the grease of the machine and taste the heavy, fertile mud of a new Bavaria.

You roll a rare, salt-grown plum across your teeth—its thin skin bursting with a sharp, engineered sweetness and the clean, cold water of the upper condenser, a costly sacrament that promises their children will never have to choke down the grey flour of the transit.

choice.text: Emphasize the potential for future prosperity and expansion to the heir.

prose: You chew a crust of dense, dark bread baked from the dregs of the simulated yeast, tasting the heavy, sour grain and the bitter salt of a community that has survived by sharing every crumb in the shadow of the boilers.

choice.text: Instill in the heir the importance of community, faith, and the values that sustained them.

decision.prompt: The sudden, green bite of dill on your tongue tastes of ancient Bavarian valleys, a sharp contrast to the cold sulfur of the transit. As this communion of the soil awakens your spirit, how will {given_name} carry the sacred spark of the {family_name} name forward?

options.text: Bind our life to the keeper of the hydroponic gardens, raising children who will learn to pray in the soil of the new world while carrying the old Bavarian bloodline forward.
Bring the orphaned acolytes of the chapel ship into our existing household, teaching them the sacred chants and stewardship of the earth, letting our spirit endure though our bloodline narrows.

Renounce all personal legacy to enter the silent Order of the Seed, committing our remaining years entirely to the sacred hydroponic soils so that future generations may eat, even if our own line ends here.
